In the liver, bilirubin is conjugated with glucuronic acid by the enzyme glucuronyltransferase, making it soluble in water. Much of it goes into the bile and thus out into the small intestine. Though most bile acid is resorbed in the terminal _____ to participate in enterohepatic circulation, _____ bilirubin is not absorbed and instead passes into the colon.

Secretion into biliary ducts is usually but not always a prelude to toxicant clearance by excretion in feces or urine. Exceptions occur when compounds such as _____ are repeatedly delivered into the intestinal lumen via bile, efficiently absorbed from the intestinal lumen, and then redirected to the liver via portal blood, a process known as enterohepatic cycling. A few compounds, such as _____, are absorbed from the biliary tract; the extensive reabsorption of this compound from the gallbladder is thought to contribute to the long biological half-life and toxicity of this toxicant.

Uptake of this toxin by hepatocytes leads to hyperphosphorylation of cytoskeletal proteins secondary to this toxin’s covalent binding to the catalytic subunit of serine/threonine protein phosphatases. Reversible phosphorylation of cytoskeletal structural and motor proteins are critical to the dynamic integrity of the cytoskeleton. Extensive hyperphosphorylation produced by large amounts of this toxin leads to marked deformation of hepatocytes due to a unique collapse of the microtubular actin scaffold into a spiny central aggregate. Identify the toxin below.

Lower doses of this toxin, insufficient to produce the gross structural deformations, diminish uptake and secretory functions of hepatocytes in association with preferential hyperphosphorylation of the cytoplasmic motor protein dynein. Dynein is a mechanicochemical protein that drives vesicles along microtubules using energy from ATP hydrolysis; central to the hydrolysis of the dynein-bound ATP is a cycle of kinase phosphorylation and phosphatase dephosphorylation. Thus, hyperphosphorylation of dynein freezes this motor pump. Chronic exposure to low levels of this toxin has raised new concerns about the health effects of this water contaminant. Specifically, low levels of this toxin promote liver

Fatty liver (steatosis) is defined biochemically as an appreciable increase in the hepatic lipid (mainly triglyceride) content, which is <5% by weight in normal human liver. Histologically, in standard paraffin-embedded and solvent-extracted sections, hepatocytes containing excess fat appear to have multiple round, empty vacuoles that displace the nucleus to the periphery of the cell. Currently, the most common cause of hepatic steatosis is:

options.text: insulin resistance due to central obesity and sedentary lifestyle

text: Hepatic fibrosis (scaring) occurs in response to chronic liver injury and is characterized by the accumulation of excessive amounts of fibrous tissue, specifically fibril forming collagens type I and III, and a decrease in normal plasma membrane collagen type IV. Fibrosis can develop around central veins and portal tracts or within the space of Disse. The excessive extracellular matrix protein deposition and the loss of sinusoidal endothelial cell fenestrae and of hepatocyte microvilli limit exchange of nutrients and waste material between hepatocytes and sinusoidal blood. With continuing collagen deposition, the architecture of the liver is disrupted by interconnecting fibrous scars. When the fibrous scars subdivide the remaining liver mass into nodules of regenerating hepatocytes, fibrosis has progressed to cirrhosis and the liver has limited residual capacity to perform its essential functions. The primary cause of hepatic fibrosis/cirrhosis in humans worldwide is viral hepatitis. However, biliary obstruction and in particular alcoholic and nonalcoholic steatohepatitis are of growing importance for the development of hepatic fibrosis. In addition, fibrosis can be induced by chronic exposure to drugs and chemicals including ethanol and by heavy metal overload. Repeated treatment with the following compound(s) has been associated with hepatic fibrosis in experimental animals and humans.
A potential explanation for the site-specific toxicity of the following compounds to zone 1 hepatocytes is the preferential update and high oxygen levels in this region.

Consistent with the critical role of protein binding for cell injury are the findings that APAP protein adducts are located only in centrilobular hepatocytes undergoing necrosis and that no APAP hepatotoxicity is observed without protein binding. Because protein binding can be prevented by conjugation of NAPQI with GSH, any manipulation that reduces hepatic GSH levels, e.g., fasting or

Although the glomerular capillary wall permits a high rate of fluid filtration (approximately 20% of blood entering the glomerulus is filtered), it provides a significant barrier to the transglomerular passage of macromolecules. Experiments using a variety of charged and neutral tracers have established that this barrier function is based on the ability of the glomerulus to act as a size-selective and charge selective filter. In general, the filtration of macromolecules is inversely proportional to the molecular weight of a substance; thus, small molecules, such as _____ (MW 5,500), are freely filtered, whereas large molecules, such as _____ (MW 56,000–70,000), are restricted.

The charge-selective properties of the glomerulus appear to be related to the _____ groups of the glomerular basement membrane coupled with the _____ coating of the epithelial and endothelial cells. These highly _____ components produce electrostatic repulsion and hinder the circulation of poly_____ macromolecules, thereby markedly retarding passage of these molecules across the filtration barrier.

Toxicants that neutralize or reduce the number of fixed _____ charges on glomerular structural elements therefore will impair the charge- and/or size-selective properties of the glomerulus, resulting in urinary excretion of poly_____ and/or _____-molecular weight proteins.

The formation of urine is a highly complex and integrated process in which the volume and composition of the glomerular filtrate is progressively altered as fluid passes through each of the different tubular segments. Which section of the nephron is considered the workhorse, as it reabsorbs approximately 60–80% of solute and water filtered at the glomerulus?

The different segments of the proximal tubule exhibit marked biochemical and physiologic heterogeneity. For example, filtered HCO3−, low-molecular-weight proteins, amino acids, and glucose are primarily reabsorbed by the _____ segment. Transport capacities for these substances in the other segments is appreciably less; for example, glucose reabsorption in the other segments is about 50% and 10% of the segment where primary reabsorption takes place. In contrast, the principal site of organic anion and cation secretion is in the _____ and _____ segments, respectively.
Catabolism and apical transport of glutathione (GSH) occurs to a much greater extent in the _____ segment, where the brush-border enzyme _____ is present in greater amounts.

The thin descending and ascending limbs and the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle are critical to the processes involved in urinary concentration. Approximately 25% of the filtered Na+ and K+ and 20% of the filtered water are reabsorbed by the segments of the loop of Henle. The tubular fluid entering the thin descending limb is iso-osmotic to the renal interstitium; water is _____ and solutes, such as electrolytes and urea, may enter from the interstitium. In contrast, the thin ascending limb is relatively _____ to water and urea, and Na+ and Cl– are reabsorbed by passive diffusion. The thick ascending limb is _____ to water, and active transport of Na+ and Cl– is mediated by the Na+/K+-2Cl– cotransport mechanism, with the energy provided by the Na+, K+-ATPase. The relatively high rates of Na+, K+-ATPase activity and oxygen demand, coupled with the meager oxygen supply in the medullary thick ascending limb, are believed to contribute to the vulnerability of this segment of the nephron to hypoxic injury.

In the Loop of Henle, the close interdependence between metabolic workload and tubular vulnerability has been demonstrated, revealing that selective damage to the thick ascending limb in the isolated perfused kidney can be blunted by reducing tubular work and oxygen consumption (via inhibition of the Na+, K+-ATPase with _____) or by increasing oxygen supply (via provision of an oxygen carrier, _____). Conversely, increasing the tubular workload (via the ionophore _____) exacerbates hypoxic injury to this segment.

Many nephrotoxicants have their primary effects on discrete segments or regions of the nephron. For example, the proximal tubule is the primary target for most nephrotoxic antibiotics, antineoplastics, halogenated hydrocarbons, mycotoxins, and heavy metals, whereas the glomerulus is the primary site for immune complexes, the loop of Henle/collecting ducts for _____ ions, and the medulla/papilla for chronically consumed _____ mixtures. The reasons underlying this site-selective injury are complex but can be attributed in part to site-specific differences in blood flow, transport and accumulation of chemicals, physicochemical properties of the epithelium, reactivity of cellular/molecular targets, balance of bioactivation/detoxification reactions, cellular energetics, and/or regenerative/repair mechanisms.

Chemical “X” is highly lipophilic and interacts with lipid sterols such as cholesterol, resulting in the formation of transmembrane channels or pores and disrupting membrane permeability. This chemical effectively transforms the tight distal tubular epithelium into one that is leaky to water and ions and impairs reabsorption at these sites. Based on this description, the most likely identity of Chemical “X” is:

Fluoride inhibits sodium chloride reabsorption in the thick ascending limb and inhibits ADH-mediated reabsorption of water, possibly due to disruption in adenylate cyclase. Nephrotoxicity caused by which of the following chemicals is associated with the inhibitory effects of the metabolite fluoride on solute and water reabsorption?

Amphotericin B reacts with plasma membrane sterols, increasing membrane permeability; fumonisin B1 inhibits sphinganine (sphingosine) N-acyltransferase; and Hg2+ binds to sulfhydryl groups on cellular proteins. In contrast, some chemicals are not toxic until they are biotransformed to a reactive intermediate. Biologically reactive intermediates, also known as alkylating agents, are electron-deficient compounds (electrophiles) that bind to cellular nucleophiles (electron-rich compounds) such as proteins and lipids. Which of the following compounds are bioactivated by cytochrome P450 to N-acetyl-p-benzoquinoneimine and phosgene, respectively?

Many metals, including cadmium, chromium, lead, mercury, platinum, and uranium, are nephrotoxic. It is important to recognize that the nature and severity of metal nephrotoxicity varies with respect to its form. For example, _____ mercury compounds produce a greater degree of renal injury and a lesser degree of neurotoxicity than do _____ mercury compounds, an effect that has been associated with the greater degree of lipophilicity of _____ mercury compounds.

Metals may cause toxicity through their ability to bind to sulfhydryl groups. Which of the following metals have an affinity for sulfhydryl groups that is very high and about ten orders of magnitude higher than their affinity carbonyl or amino groups? These metals may cause renal cellular injury through their ability to bind to sulfhydryl groups of critical proteins within the cells and thereby inhibit their normal function.

Which metal exhibits the characteristics described in the following narrative? This metal has a half-life of greater than 10 years in humans and thus accumulates in the body over time. Approximately 50% of the body burden can be found in the kidney and nephrotoxicity can be observed when concentrations exceed 50 μg/gm kidney wet weight. Metallothioneins are a family of low-molecular-weight, cysteine-rich metal-binding proteins that have a high affinity for this metal and other heavy metals. In general, the mechanism by which metallothionein is thought to play a role in heavy metal toxicity is through its ability to bind to a heavy metal and thereby render it biologically inactive. This assumes that the unbound or “free” concentration of the metal is the toxic species. Inside the tubular cells it is thought that lysosomal degradation of the metal-metallothionein complex results in the release of “free” this metal, which, in turn, induces renal metallothionein production. Once the renal metallothionein pool is saturated, the “free” metal initiates injury. It is also likely that this metal is reabsorbed luminally and basolaterally as a cysteine conjugate. The mechanism by which this metal produces injury at the cellular level is not clear; however, low concentrations of this metal have been shown to interfere with the normal function of several cellular signal transduction pathways.

Chemical “X” produces nephrotoxicity in a variety of species, with some species being more sensitive than others. The primary cellular target is the proximal tubule, with no primary damage to the glomerulus or the distal tubule. Proteinuria, glucosuria, and increased BUN levels are all characteristic of Chemical “X”-induced nephrotoxicity. The nephrotoxicity produced by Chemical “X” is linked to its metabolism by renal cytochrome P450 and the formation of a reactive intermediate that binds covalently to nucleophilic groups on cellular macromolecules. Cytochrome P450 biotransforms Chemical “X” to trichloromethanol, which is unstable and releases HCl to form phosgene. Phosgene can react with (1) water to produce 2HCl + CO2, (2) two molecules of glutathione to produce diglutathionyl dithiocarbonate, (3) cysteine to produce 2-oxothizolidine-4-carboxylic acid, or (4) cellular macromolecules to initiate toxicity. Which of the following chemicals is Chemical “X”?

Acute exposures of Toxin “Y” produce similar effects on the kidney as Toxin “X”; however, chronic exposures to Toxin “Y” result in progressive tubular atrophy and fibrosis. One or both of these mycotoxins have been implicated in Balkan endemic nephropathy (BEN) in humans, although the data to support this are less than clear. Which of the following substances is Toxin “Y”, which is primarily implicated in BEN?

These compounds are highly polar cations; they are almost exclusively filtered by the glomerulus and excreted unchanged. Once filtered, these compounds undergo proximal tubular reabsorption by binding to anionic phospholipids in the brush border, followed by endocytosis and sequestration in lysosomes of the S1 and S2 segments of proximal tubules. Basolateral membrane binding and uptake also may occur, but this is a minor contribution to the total proximal tubular uptake of these compounds. The earliest lesion observed following clinically relevant doses of these compounds is an increase in the size and number of lysosomes. These lysosomes contain myeloid bodies, which are electron-dense lamellar structures containing undegraded phospholipids. The renal phospholipidosis produced by these compounds is thought to occur through their inhibition of lysosomal hydrolases, such as sphingomyelinase and phospholipases. Which of the following classes of compounds does the above narrative describe?

Drug “X” is a very effective antifungal agent whose clinical utility is limited by its nephrotoxicity. Renal dysfunction associated with Drug “X” treatment is dependent on cumulative dose and is due to both hemodynamic and tubular effects. With respect to hemodynamics, Drug “X” administration is associated with decreases in RBF and GFR secondary to renal arteriolar vasoconstriction or activation of TGF. Drug “X” nephrotoxicity is characterized by ADH-resistant polyuria, renal tubular acidosis, hypokalemia, and either acute or chronic renal failure. Drug “X” nephrotoxicity is unusual in that it impairs the functional integrity of the glomerulus and of the proximal and distal portions of the nephron. Which of the following substances is Drug “X”?

Drug “X” is an important immunosuppressive agent and is widely used to prevent graft rejection in organ transplantation. Drug “X” is a fungal cyclic polypeptide and acts by selectively inhibiting T-cell activation. Nephrotoxicity is a critical side effect of Drug “X”, with nearly all patients who receive the drug exhibiting some form of nephrotoxicity. Clinically, Drug “X”-induced nephrotoxicity may manifest as (1) acute reversible renal dysfunction, (2) acute vasculopathy, and (3) chronic nephropathy with interstitial fibrosis. Which of the following substances is Drug “X”?

Drug “X” is a valuable drug in the treatment of solid tumors, with nephrotoxicity limiting its clinical use. The kidney is not only responsible for the majority of Drug “X” excreted but is also the primary site of accumulation. The effects of Drug “X” on the kidney are several, including acute and chronic renal failure, renal magnesium wasting, and polyuria and patients treated with cisplatin regimens permanently lose 10–30% of their renal function. Early effects of Drug “X” are decreases in RBF and GFR produced by vasoconstriction and is followed by tubular injury with enzymuria. The early polyuria, has been suggested, to result from the inhibition of vasopressin release. Although the primary cellular target associated with ARF is the proximal tubule S3 segment in the rat, in humans the S1 and S2 segments, distal tubule, and collecting ducts can also be affected. The chronic renal failure observed with Drug “X” is due to prolonged exposure and is characterized by focal necrosis in numerous segments of the nephron without a significant effect on the glomerulus. Which of the following substances is Drug “X”?

The proximal airways (_____ and _____) of humans have a pseudostratified epithelium containing ciliated cells and two types of nonciliated cells: mucous and serous cells. Mucous cells (and glandular structures) produce respiratory tract mucus, a family of high-molecular-weight glycoproteins with a sugar content of 80% or more. They coat the epithelium with a viscoelastic sticky protective layer that traps pollutants and cell debris. Serous cells produce a fluid in which mucus may be dissolved, or upon which a mucus layer may be floated. The action of the respiratory tract cilia continuously drives the mucus layer toward the _____, where it is removed from the respiratory system by swallowing or expectoration. The mucus layer is also thought to have antioxidant, acid-neutralizing, and free radical scavenging functions that protect the epithelial cells.

Conducting airways have a characteristic branched bifurcating structure, with successive airway generations containing approximately twice the number of bronchi progressively decreasing in internal diameter. Thus, the conducting airways contain a continuously increasing total surface area from the trachea to the distal airways. Bifurcations are flow dividers and airway branch points serve as sites of impaction for particles. Successively narrower diameters also favor the collection of gases and particles on airway walls. Eventually a transition zone is reached where cartilaginous airways (_____) give way to noncartilaginous airways (_____), which in turn give way to gas exchange regions, respiratory bronchioles, and alveoli. In the bronchiolar epithelium, mucus-producing cells and glands give way to _____ cells.

Gas exchange occurs in the alveoli, which comprise approximately 80–90% of the total parenchymal lung volume; adult human lungs contain an estimated 300 million alveoli. The ratio of total capillary surface to total alveolar surface is slightly less than 1. Capillaries, blood plasma, and formed blood elements are separated from the air space by a thin layer of tissue formed by _____, interstitial, and endothelial components. Type I and type II alveolar cells represent approximately 25% of all the cells in the alveolar septum.

In the alveoli, _____ cells are cuboidal and show abundant perinuclear cytoplasm. They produce surfactant and, in the case of damage to the _____ epithelium, may undergo mitotic division and replace damaged cells. _____ cells cover a large surface area (approximately 90% of the alveolar surface). They have an attenuated cytoplasm and appear to be poor in organelles but probably are as metabolically competent as are the more compact _____ cells. Preferential damage to _____ cells by various agents may be explained by the fact that they constitute a large percentage of the total target (surface of the epithelium).

options.text: type II/type I/type I/type II/type I
type I/type II/type I/type II/type I
type II/type I/type II/type I/type II
type I/type II/type II/type I/type II

text: The total volume of air in an inflated human lung, approximately 5700 cm3, represents the _____.
After a maximum expiration, the lung retains approximately 1200 cm3 of air, the _____.
The air volume moved into and out of the lung with a maximum inspiratory and expiratory movement, which is called the _____, is thus approximately 4500 cm3.
Under resting conditions, only a fraction of the vital capacity, _____, is moved into and out of the lung. In resting humans, it measures approximately 500 cm3 with each breath.
In emphysema, the alveoli _____ and _____ air is trapped. While the total lung capacity (TLC) may stay the same or even increase, the volume of air that is actually moved during breathing is _____.

The lung receives the entire output from the _____, approximately 70–80 cm3 of blood per heartbeat, and thus may be exposed to substantial amounts of toxic agents carried in the blood. A chemical placed onto or deposited under the skin (_____) or introduced directly into a peripheral vein (_____) travels through the venous system to the right ventricle and then comes into contact with the pulmonary capillary bed before distribution to other organs or tissues in the body.

Gas exchange takes place across the entire alveolar surface. Contact to an airborne toxic chemical thus occurs over a surface of approximately 140 m2. This surface area is second only to the _____ (approximately 250m2) and considerably larger than the _____ (approximately 1.75 m2), two other organs that are in direct contact with the outside world.

The _____ has the most P-450. The amount of total lung P-450 contributed by these cells is species dependent, with humans having _____ P-450 in their lungs in these types of cells than rats or mice. Further, the isoforms of P-450 present and their location also vary by species.

The nasal epithelia metabolizes foreign compounds via the cytochrome P-450 monooxygenase system. Cytochrome P-450s are expressed at some of the highest levels for an extrahepatic tissue in the nasal mucosa and this pattern of expression varies by nasal region and cell type. The olfactory mucosa is considered to be a “metabolic hot spot.” Most species have P-450 in nasal tissue and some of these P-450s are predominantly, possibly even solely, expressed in the olfactory mucosa (e.g., CYP2G1, CYP2A3, and CYP2A13). Metabolism by the olfactory epithelium may play a role in providing, or preventing, access of inhalants directly to the brain; for example, inhaled _____ may be converted to metabolites that move to the brain by axonal transport. The presence of the following cytochrome P-450 isozymes in the respiratory tract of at least one species has been reported: CYP1A1, CYP1B1, CYP2A3, CYP2A10/2A11, CYP2B1/4, CYP2B6, CYP2B7, CYP2E1, CYP2F1/2/4, CYP2S1, CYP2J2, CYP2G1, CYP3A, and CYP4B1.

Flavin monooxygenase activity (FMO1 and FMO2) is found in rodent and human lung and nasal tissue. The isoforms present in the lung (_____) are different from that found in the liver (_____). _____ is the predominant isoform in the nasal mucosa.
The sites of deposition of gases in the respiratory tract define the pattern of toxicity of those gases. _____ is the critical factor in determining how deeply a given gas penetrates into the lung.

Highly _____ gases such as _____ do not penetrate farther than the nose unless doses are very high, and are therefore relatively nontoxic to animals, especially obligatory nose breathers, such as the rat. Relatively _____ gases such as ozone and _____ penetrate deeply into the lung and reach the smallest airways and the alveoli (centriacinar region), where they can elicit toxic responses.

_____ is usually the critical factor that determines the region of the respiratory tract in which a particle or an aerosol will be deposited. Deposition of particles on the surface of the lung and airways is brought about by a combination of lung anatomy and the patterns of airflow in the respiratory system.

The median diameter that is determined may reflect the number of particles, as in the count median diameter (CMD), or reflect mass, as in the mass median aerodynamic diameter (MMAD). If more particles actually reach the deep lung, the higher is the probability of a toxic effect. Particle surface area is of special importance when toxic materials are adsorbed on particles and thus are carried into the lung. Large particles (larger than 5 μm MMAD) are usually trapped in the upper respiratory tract (nasopharyngeal region and large conducting airways), whereas smaller particles (0.2–5 μm MMAD) can be transported to the smaller airways and the alveoli. Inhaled ambient particles and aerosols are most frequently _____ in size. The size distribution of many aerosols approximates a log-normal distribution that may be described by the median or geometric mean and the geometric standard deviation.

_____ is the proper measurement for particles that are deposited by impaction and sedimentation. For very small particles, which are deposited primarily by _____, the critical factor is _____, not density. It must be kept in mind that the size of a particle may change before its deposition in the respiratory tract. Materials that are hygroscopic, such as sodium chloride, sulfuric acid, and glycerol, take on water and grow in size in the warm, saturated atmosphere of the upper and lower respiratory tract.
_____ occurs when the trajectory of a particle brings it near enough to a surface so that an edge of the particle contacts the airway surface.

Interception is important for the deposition of fibers. Whereas fiber diameter determines the probability of deposition by impaction and sedimentation, interception is dependent on fiber length. Thus, a fiber with a diameter of 1 μm and a length of 200 μm will be deposited in the bronchial tree primarily by _____ rather than _____.

The term asbestos describes silicate minerals in fiber form. The most commonly mined and commercially used asbestos fibers include the serpentine chrysotile asbestos and the amphiboles crocidolite, anthophyllite, amosite, actinolite, and tremolite. The hazards associated with asbestos exposure depend on fiber length. Fibers 2 μm in length may produce _____; _____ is associated with fibers 5 μm long, and _____ with fibers larger than 10 μm.

Fiber diameter is another critical feature of the hazards from asbestos fibers. Fibers with diameters larger than approximately 3 μm do not readily penetrate into the peripheral lung. For the development of _____, fiber diameter must be less than 0.5 μm, because thinner fibers may be translocated from their site of deposition via the lymphatics to other organs, including the pleural surface. Once asbestos fibers have been deposited in the lung, they may become phagocytized by alveolar macrophages. _____ fibers are completely ingested and subsequently removed via the mucociliary escalator. _____ fibers are incompletely ingested, and the macrophages become unable to leave the alveoli. Activated by the fibers, macrophages release mediators such as lymphokines and growth factors, which in turn attract immunocompetent cells or stimulate collagen production.

Crystalline silica is a major component of the earth’s crust; after oxygen, silicon is the most common element. As a pure mineral, silicon exists primarily in the form of its dioxide, silica (SiO2), which has a crystalline form in which a central silicon atom forms a tetrahedron with four shared oxygen atoms. The three principal crystalline isomeric forms are quartz, tridymite, and cristobalite. The tetrahedral structure is linked to fibrogenic potential. In studies with humans, the most fibrogenic particle

The pathophysiological basis of pulmonary fibrosis in chronic silicosis is probably better understood than is the etiology of any other form of lung fibrosis. Which of the following events has been established as an initiating event for silica-induced pulmonary fibrosis?

options.text: ingestion of silica by pulmonary macrophages
susceptibility of the host’s immune response

text: In experimental animals, inhaled or parenterally administered naphthalene has shown remarkable species and tissue specificity: it produces extensive and selective necrosis in the bronchiolar epithelium of some species. Animals treated with small doses of naphthalene along with inhibitors of cytochrome P-450s show little or no tissue damage, implicating metabolism in the toxicity of this chemical. Metabolism to the naphthalene oxide is mediated primarily through CYP2F2. Which of the following species are most susceptible to naphthalene-induced toxicity due to predominance of this metabolic pathway?

Chemical “X” is widely used as an anticancer and immunosuppressive drug. The undesirable side effects include hemorrhagic cystitis and pulmonary fibrosis. Chemical X is metabolized by the cytochrome P-450 system to two highly reactive metabolites: acrolein and phosphoramide mustard. In the lung, cooxidation with the prostaglandin H synthase system, which has high activity in the lung, is a possibility. Identify chemical “X” from the following list of substances.

Chemical “X” is an effective chemotherapeutic agent that exerts its antitumor properties by reacting with cellular macromolecules and forms inter- and intrastrand cross-links with DNA. In humans, a dose-related pulmonary toxicity is often noticed first by a decrease in diffusion capacity. Pulmonary fibrosis caused by this drug can be fatal. The mechanism of action is not entirely clear. It is possible that this compound inhibits pulmonary glutathione disulfide reductase, an event that may lead to a disturbed GSH/GSSG state in pulmonary cells. Eventually, this state leaves the cell unable to cope with oxidant stress. Identify chemical “X” from the following list of substances.

Chemical “X”, a mixture of several structurally similar compounds, is a widely used cancer chemotherapeutic agent. Pulmonary fibrosis, often fatal, represents the most serious form of toxicity. The sequence of damage includes necrosis of capillary endothelial and type I alveolar cells, edema formation and hemorrhage, delayed (after 1–2 weeks) proliferation of type II epithelial cells, and eventually thickening of the alveolar walls by fibrotic changes. In many tissues, a cytosolic hydrolase inactivates chemical X. In lung and skin, two target organs for chemical X toxicity, the activity of this enzyme is low compared with that in other organs. Chemical X stimulates the production of collagen in the lung. It also combines with Fe (II) and molecular oxygen; when it combines with DNA, single- and double-strand breaks are produced by a free radical reaction. Animal models of chemical X-induced pulmonary fibrosis have been used to study the efficacy of promising antifibrotic drugs. Identify chemical “X” from the following list of substances.

Compound “X” has been reported to cause pulmonary fibrosis. Specifically, the fibrosis that may occur from occupational exposures to abrasives of this compound are known as “Shavers disease”, “corundum smelter’s lung”, and “bauxite lung”. Compound X is also associated with neuronal injury (neuronopathy). The cellular basis of the neurotoxicity includes: spongiosis cortex, neurofibrillary aggregates, and degenerative changes in the cortex. The clinical neurologic findings include: dementia, encephalopathy (humans), and learning deficits. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” has been known and used since ancient times as the Poison of Kings and the King of Poisons. The biological half-life of methylated forms of compound X is in the range of 30 hours. Compound X has a predilection for skin and is excreted by desquamation of skin and in sweat, particularly during periods of profuse sweating. It also concentrates in forming fingernails and hair. Compound X exposure produces characteristic transverse white bands across fingernails (Mees’ line), which appear about 6 weeks after the onset of symptoms of compound X toxicity. The skin is a major target organ in chronic inorganic forms of compound X exposure. In humans, chronic exposure induces a series of characteristic changes in skin epithelium. Diffuse or spotted hyperpigmentation and, alternatively, hypopigmentation can first appear between 6 months to 3 years with chronic exposure to inorganic forms of compound X. Palmar-plantar hyperkeratosis usually follows the initial appearance of compound X-induced pigmentation changes within a period of years. Compound X is a known human carcinogen. It is also a known to cause neuronal injury. The cellular basis of the neurotoxicity includes: brain swelling and hemorrhage (acute), axonal degeneration in the peripheral nervous system (chronic). The clinical neurological findings include: encephalopathy (acute) and peripheral neuropathy (chronic). Treatment for compound X poisoning includes the oral chelator penicillamine or succimer (2,3-dimercaptosuccinic acid, DMSA). Identify compound X from the following:

Trivalent insoluble salts of compound “X” are used for various gastrointestinal disorders including diarrhea, flatulence, constipation, cramps, and dyspepsia. Colloidal subcitrate or subsalicylate forms of compound X are widely used to treat peptic ulcer and Helicobacter pylori associated gastritis. Compound X-containing medications are consumed worldwide and the risk of compound X-related toxicity in the general population is relatively low. The main target organs for compound X toxicity are kidney, brain, and bone. Most forms of compound X are insoluble and poorly absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract or when applied to the skin. British anti-lewisite (BAL) is the only chelator effective in lowering brain concentrations compound X. Compound X is associated with neuronal injury (neuronopathy). The cellular basis of the neurotoxicity includes: neuronal loss in the basal ganglia and Purkinje cells of the cerebellum. The clinical neurological findings include: emotional disturbances, encephalopathy, and myoclonus. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” is an essential metal required for many metabolic and cellular functions. Iron and compound X can compete for the same binding protein in serum (transferrin) and the same transport systems (divalent metal transporter, DMT1). Inhalation of particulate compound X may result in direct transfer to brain tissue via the olfactory system. Compound X concentrates in mitochondria. Compound X readily crosses the blood–brain barrier and accumulates in specific brain regions. Chronic compound X-induced neurotoxicity is of great concern and the brain is considered the most sensitive organ to compound X. Neurotoxicity is associated with elevated brain levels of compound X, primarily in those areas known to contain high concentrations of non-heme iron, such as the substantia nigra, basal ganglia, caudate putamen, globus pallidus, and subthalamic nuclei. Early manifestations of compound X neurotoxicity include headache, insomnia, memory loss, muscle cramps, and emotional instability. Initial outward symptoms progress gradually and are mainly psychiatric. As exposure continues and the disease progresses, patients may develop prolonged muscle contractions (dystonia), decreased muscle movement (hypokinesia), rigidity, hand tremor, speech disturbances, and festinating “cock-walk” gait. These signs are associated with damage to dopaminergic neurons that control muscle movement. Specialized T1-weighted magnetic resonance brain imaging of patients with compound X-induced neurotoxicity indicate high levels in the basal ganglia and especially in the globus pallidus. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” is a potent liver toxicant. It is bioactivated via CYP2E1. It can also cause neuronal injury (neuronopathy). The cellular basis for neurotoxicity includes: enlarged astrocytes in the striatum and globus pallidus. The clinical neurological findings include encephalopathy, which is secondary to liver failure. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” is an inducer of the CYP3A4 and CYP2C19 families of the P450 enzyme responsible for the liver’s degradation of various drugs. Compound X is an antiepileptic and is believed to protect against seizures by causing voltage-dependent block of voltage gated sodium channels. This blocks sustained high frequency repetitive firing of action potentials. Compound X elimination kinetics show mixed-order behavior at therapeutic concentrations. A small increase in dose may lead to a large increase in drug concentration as elimination becomes saturated. Compound X is a known teratogen. It is also known to cause neurotoxicity. The cellular basis of the neurotoxicity includes degeneration of Purkinje cells (cerebellum). The clinical neurologic findings include: nystagmus, ataxia, and dizziness. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” is an antibiotic drug. It was the first effective treatment for tuberculosis. Compound X is a protein synthesis inhibitor. It binds to the small 16S rRNA of the 30S subunit of the bacterial ribosome, interfering with the binding of formyl-methionyl-tRNA to the 30S subunit. The vestibular portion of cranial nerve VIII (the vestibulococlear nerve) can be affected by compound X, resulting in tinnitus, vertigo and ataxia. It can also lead to nephrotoxicity. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” is produced by certain plants and fungi. It is a potent mitochondrial inhibitor (complex II), and toxic to humans. Compound X is an excitotoxin and has been shown to cause brain lesions similar to those of Huntington′s disease. The cellular basis of neurotoxicity includes necrosis of the basal ganglia. The clinical neurologic findings include: seizures, delayed dystonia/grimacing. Fatalities after eating moldy sugarcane have been linked to compound X toxicity. Ruminants grazing in regions with compound X-producing plants acquire resistance because of reduction of the nitro group to an amine by ruminal bacteria. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” is one of the most toxic metals. The odorless and tasteless sulfate form was once widely used as rat poison and ant killer. Compound X is absorbed through the skin and gastrointestinal tract. The triad of gastroenteritis, polyneuropathy, and alopecia is regarded as the classic syndrome of compound X poisoning. After oral ingestion of compound X, gastrointestinal symptoms occur, including nausea, vomiting, gastroenteritis, abdominal pain, and gastrointestinal hemorrhage. Neurological symptoms usually appear 2–5 days after acute exposure, depending on the age and the level of exposure. A consistent and characteristic feature of compound X intoxication in humans is the extreme sensitivity of the legs, followed by the “burning feet syndrome” and paresthesia. Central nervous system toxicity is manifest by hallucinations, lethargy, delirium, convulsions, and coma. Therapy for compound X intoxication combines forced diuresis, use of activated charcoal, prevention of reabsorption by administration of Prussian blue, and administration of potassium ferric hexacyanoferrate. Prussian blue is the recommended drug of choice in acute compound X poisoning. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” can be formed as a by-product during the cooking of starchy foods (including French fries, potato chips, and bread). Compound X is considered a potential occupational carcinogen by U.S. government agencies and classified as a Group 2A carcinogen by the IARC. Compound X is metabolized by direct conjugation with glutathione or oxidation to compound “Y”, which undergo further metabolism and are excreted in urine. The mechanism underlying neurotoxic effects of compound “X” involves interference with the kinesin-related motor proteins in nerve cells or with fusion proteins in the formation of vesicles at the nerve terminus and eventual cell death. Compound Y is a reactive electrophile with clear evidence of carcinogenic activity in female and male rats and mice. The basis of neurotoxicity for compound X includes: axonal degeneration with the axon terminal affected in the earliest stages. The neurological findings include peripheral neuropathy, often sensory. Identify compound X from the following:

Significant safety data on compound “X” comes from its use in the viscose rayon industry. The basis of neurotoxicity for compound X includes axonal degeneration with early stages including neurofilamentous swelling, most likely from cross-linking with adjacent protein nucleophiles. The neurologic findings include psychosis (acute) and peripheral neuropathy (chronic). Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” is a neurotoxic compound that causes both a proximal neurofilamentous axonopathy and loss of the vestibular sensory hair cells. It has also been shown to cause a “waltzing syndrome” (vertical and lateral head movements, circling, and retropulsion). Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” is a highly water‐soluble vitamin that plays an important part in the functioning of many enzymes, especially those involved in amino acid metabolism. Compound X toxicity is a recognized cause of sensory neuropathy. It can occur with chronic use of compound X supplementation over several years, and also with acute over‐dosage with parenteral compound X. It is used to produce an animal model of sensory neuropathy, because unlike drugs such as cisplatin, which also cause sensory neuropathy, it does not cause systemic toxicity. Identify compound X from the following:

Alcohol potentiates the effects of compound “X” on the central nervous system (CNS). Concurrent alcohol consumption and exposure to compound X can result in “degreaser’s flush,” a temporary redness and itching of the back, neck, and face. Theoretically, liver dysfunction or disulfiram (Antabuse) treatment could reduce the metabolism of compound X and thus increase its CNS depressant effects. Identify compound X from the following:
The clinical syndrome associated with exposure to methylphenyltetrahydropyridine (MPTP) is:
The blood brain barrier includes which of the following:

options.text: foot processes of astrocytes and capillary endothelial cells

text: How do P-glycoproteins on brain capillary endothelial cells affect the CNS accumulation of digoxin, vinca alkaloids, and cyclosporine A?

options.text: enhance metabolism and reduce available xenobiotic to cross into CNS
reduce metabolism and enhance available xenobiotic to cross into CNS

text: Exposure to methyl mercury causes fetal neuronal injury due to:

options.text: fetal inability to metabolize and excrete methyl mercury
incomplete development of the blood brain barrier
failure of the material fetal barrier

text: Xenobiotics with a high octanol/water partition coefficient are more likely to:
Chronic nitrous oxide abuse produces:

options.text: peripheral neuropathy with posterior column involvement
narcolepsy and severe, life-threatening withdrawal syndrome

text: Glycine inhibition results in:

options.text: reduction of symptoms of sedative hypnotic withdrawal

text: Patients with trauma may experience neuropsychiatric disorders as a result of increased penetration of xenobiotics due to:

Compound “X” is a biocide that has antibacterial and antifungal properties. It is the active ingredient in shampoos and other preparations for the treatment of seborrheic dermatitis and dandruff. Compound X is also used as an antifouling agent for ship paints, drywall, and tarps, and as an antibacterial agent for incorporation into cleaning sponges. Because the compound is directly applied to the human scalp in antidandruff shampoos, the finding that compound X produced limb weakness and peripheral neuropathy in rodents after oral administration raised concern about potential neurotoxicity in humans. Rats, rabbits, and guinea pigs all develop a distal axonopathy when exposed to compound X in the diet. Fortunately, however, dermal absorption of compound X is minimal, and there have been no reports of neurological findings in humans attributable to occupational or consumer compound X exposures. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” is a medication most commonly used to treat gout. It inhibits microtubule polymerization by binding to tubulin, one of the main constituents of microtubules. Availability of tubulin is essential to mitosis, so compound X effectively functions as a “mitotic poison” or spindle poison. Side effects include gastrointestinal upset and neutropenia. High doses can also damage bone marrow, lead to anemia, and cause hair loss. All of these side effects can result from hyperinhibition of mitosis. A main side effect associated with all mitotic inhibitors is peripheral neuropathy, which is a numbness or tingling in the hands and feet due to peripheral nerve damage. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” is a vinca alkaloid that can be obtained from the Madagascar periwinkle Catharanthus roseus. It is used as a chemotherapy medication to treat a number of types of cancer, including: acute lymphocytic leukemia, acute myeloid leukemia, Hodgkin’s disease, neuroblastoma, and small cell lung cancer. It is given intravenously. Nearly all evidence of compound X-induced neuropathy has been observed in humans. Most treated patients develop neurotoxicity to some extent, beginning with parasthesias of the fingers. General weakness and clumsiness is common, but this improves quickly with removal of treatment. Parasthesias may persist, however, and some distal sensory loss may be permanent. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X”, another plant alkaloid, has become a popular chemotherapeutic agent used to treat a variety of neoplasms. However, side effects include a predominantly sensory neuropathy, beginning in the hands and feet. Like vinca alkaloids, compound X binds to tubulin; however, instead of leading to depolymerization, it promotes the formation of microtubules. Once formed, these microtubules remain stabilized by compound X even in conditions that normally lead to dissociation of tubulin subunits, including cold temperatures or the presence of calcium. When compound X is injected directly into the sciatic nerve of rats, microtubules aggregate along the axon, causing axonal degeneration, demyelination, and impairment of regeneration. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” resulted in human neurotoxicity when newborn infants, particularly premature infants, were bathed with the compound to avoid staphylococcal skin infections. Following skin absorption of this hydrophobic compound, compound X enters the nervous system and results in intramyelinic edema, splitting the intraperiod line of myelin in both the CNS and the PNS. The intramyelinic edema leads to the formation of vacuoles, creating a “spongiosis” of the brain. Compound X binds tightly to cell membranes, resulting in the loss of ion gradients across the membrane. This loss of the ability to exclude ions from between the layers of myelin leads to water and ion entry, which separates the myelin layers as “edema.” Another, perhaps related, effect is the uncoupling of mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation by compound X. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” is a class III antiarrhythmic agent used for various types of cardiac dysrhythmias, both ventricular and atrial. It prolongs phase 3 of the cardiac action potential, the repolarization phase where there is normally decreased calcium permeability and increased potassium permeability. Compound X is extensively metabolized in the liver by CYP3A4, and can affect the metabolism of numerous other drugs. It interacts with digoxin, warfarin, phenytoin, and others. The metabolism of compound X is inhibited by grapefruit juice, leading to elevated serum levels of compound X. Corneal micro-deposits (cornea verticillata, also called vortex or whorl keratopathy) are almost universally present (over 90 %) in individuals taking compound X longer than 6 months, especially doses greater than 400 mg/day. These deposits typically do not cause any symptoms. Long-term administration of chemical X has been associated with peripheral neuropathies. The basis of the neurotoxicity includes: axonal degeneration and demyelination and lipid-laden lysosomes in Schwann cells. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” is a prophylactic antianginal agent used primarily in Australia and New Zealand. It is thought to act by inhibiting mitochondrial carnitine palmitoyltransferase-1. This shifts myocardial metabolism from fatty acid to glucose utilization which results in increased ATP production for the same O2 consumption and consequently increases myocardial efficiency. The major route of compound X metabolism in humans is hydroxylation by microsomal CYP2D6. The adverse effects of compound X have been seen in more than 60% of recipients in some clinical trials. The most commonly reported adverse effects include headache, dizziness, nausea and vomiting. Compound X is also known to cause hepatotoxicity and peripheral neuropathy at plasma concentrations >0.6mg/L. The symptoms of peripheral neuropathy include weakness or sensory loss and pain in the arms, hand, legs, and feet. Histological investigations indicate the development of phospholipidosis, with scaly inclusions in hepatocytes, Schwann cells and other tissues, which could point to the cause of peripheral neuropathy. The peripheral neuropathy is often but not always permanent. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” is an intercalating agent commonly used as a fluorescent tag (nucleic acid stain) in molecular biology laboratories for techniques such as agarose gel electrophoresis. Compound X is thought to act as a mutagen because it intercalates double stranded DNA (i.e., inserts itself between the strands), deforming the DNA. This could affect DNA biological processes, like DNA replication and transcription. In experimental animals, compound X has been associated with injury to myelin. The basis of neurotoxicity includes: intramyelinic edema and spongiosis of white matter. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” is an antimicrobial, antiprotozoal agent that is commonly used for the treatment of a wide variety of infections. It is listed by the US National Toxicology Program (NTP) as reasonably anticipated to be a human carcinogen. Compound X is readily reduced to the highly reactive and toxic hydroxylamine intermediate and binds to cellular macromolecules including proteins and DNA. It also inhibits CYP2C9 and CYP3A4, so may interact with medications metabolized by these enzymes (e.g. lomitapide, warfarin). Prolonged treatment with compound X is associated with a peripheral neuropathy characterized by paraesthesias and dysaesthesias. In addition, headaches, glossitis, urticaria, pruritis, and other somatosensory disorders are also seen. Long-term administration of compound X produces an irreversible sensorimotor deficit in the lower extremities of humans. Identify compound X from the following:

This class of compounds is used as synthetic intermediates in the industrial production of dyes, plastics, and explosives. Compound “X” belongs to this class and has been shown to exhibit species-specific susceptibility to its adverse effects. For example, the Sprague-Dawley rat is more susceptible to compound X-induced testicular toxicity and methemoglobinemia than the golden Syrian hamster. Compound X is also neurotoxic and produces gliovascular lesions that specifically target astrocytes in the periaqueductal gray matter of the brainstem and deep cerebellar roof nuclei. Though the molecular basis for the remarkable sensitivity of this cell population is unclear, it has been proposed that bioactivation of compound X by NADPH-dependent cytochrome c reductase and subsequent induction of oxidative stress underlies its toxicity. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” is an irreversible inhibitor of the astrocyte-specific enzyme, glutamine synthetase (GS). Ingestion of large amounts of compound X leads to neuronal cell loss in the hippocampal fascia dentata and pyramidal cell layer, in the short association fibers and lower layers of the cerebral cortex, and in cerebellar Purkinje cells. Compound X also leads to large increases of glycogen levels, primarily within astrocytic cell bodies, as well as swollen and damaged astrocytic mitochondria. Although it is generally accepted that compound X inhibits GS, it remains unclear whether this inhibition represents the primary mechanism of compound X-induced neurotoxicity. The relationship between inhibition of GS by compound X and seizure generation is also not well understood but is believed to be associated with inhibition of glutamate and GABA in seizure generation, since glutamine provides the precursor for these neurotransmitters. Identify compound X from the following:

Mitochondrial complex I inhibitors, such as MPP+ and rotenone, damage nigral neurons by mechanisms involving oxidation. Oxidative damage also plays a significant role in DAergic neuronal cell death induced by intracranial injection of compound “X”, an experimental model of Parkinson’s Disease (PD). Both enzymatic and auto-oxidation of compound X generate ROS, including H2O2, superoxide ions, and hydroxyl radicals (reactive oxygen species, or ROS). These ROS and the direct inhibition of complex I lead to lipid peroxidation, protein denaturation, and a decrease in reduced glutathione: all hallmark features of post mortem PD. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” is a natural marine acid present in some seaweed. It is a potent neuroexcitatory amino acid that acts by activating receptors for glutamate, the principal excitatory neurotransmitter in the central nervous system. Like glutamate, compound X selectively injures dendrites and neurons and shows no substantial effect on glia or axons. As a result, this compound has found use in neurobiology as a tool. Injected into a region of the brain, compound X can destroy the neurons of that area without disrupting the fibers that pass through the same region. Neurobiologists, with the help of this neurotoxic tool, are able to study the role of neurons in a particular area independent of the axonal injuries that occur when similar lesioning experiments are performed by mechanical cutting. Identify compound X from the following:

On August 18, 1961, in Capitola and Santa Cruz, California, there was an invasion of what people described as chaotic seabirds. These birds were believed to be under the influence of compound “X”, and it inspired a scene in Alfred Hitchcock’s feature film “The Birds” (1963). Compound X is a neurotoxin that causes amnesic shellfish poisoning (ASP). It is produced by algae and accumulates in shellfish, sardines, and anchovies. The effects of compound X have been attributed to several mechanisms, but the one of concern is through glutamate receptors. Compound X is an excitatory amino acid analogue of glutamate; a neurotransmitter in the brain that activates glutamate receptors. Compound X has a very strong affinity for these receptors, which results in excitotoxicity initiated by an integrative action on ionotropic glutamate receptors at both sides of the synapse blocking the channel from rapid desensitization. In addition, there is a synergistic effect with endogenous glutamate and N-Methyl-D-aspartate receptor agonists that contribute to the excitotoxicity. In the brain, compound X especially damages the hippocampus and amygdaloid nucleus. Identify compound X from the following:

Cycads are primitive plants that contain several toxins, including cycasin and compound “X”. Cycasin, which is metabolized to methylazoxymethanol (MAM), is known to be carcinogenic and cause hepatotoxicity. Compound X can act as an excitotoxin on glutamate receptors. Compound X may also misincorporate into nascent proteins in place of L-serine, possibly causing protein misfolding and aggregation, both hallmarks of tangle diseases, including Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). Compound X is considered a possible cause of the amyotrophic lateral sclerosis/parkinsonism–dementia complex (ALS/PDC) that had an extremely high rate of incidence among the Chamorro people of Guam. The Chamorro call the condition lytico-bodig. In the 1950s, ALS/PDC prevalence ratios and death rates for Chamorro residents of Guam and Rota were 50–100 times that of developed countries, including the United States. In addition to eating the seeds directly, compound X may be ingested by humans through biomagnification. Flying foxes, a Chamorro delicacy, may feed on cycad seeds and concentrate the compound X in their flesh. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” is a potent neurotoxin and the best-known paralytic shellfish toxin (PST). Ingestion of compound X, usually by consumption of shellfish contaminated by toxic algal blooms, is responsible for the human illness known as paralytic shellfish poisoning (PSP). Compound X acts as a selective sodium channel blocker. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” is the active compound found in scorpion venom, and is primarily toxic because of its ability to inhibit the conductance of chloride channels. Ingestion of lethal volumes of compound X results in paralysis through this ion channel disruption. Identify compound X from the following:

This category of poisons is produced by the marine cone snail, and are capable of inhibiting the activity of a number of ion channels such as calcium, sodium, or potassium channels. In many cases, the toxins released by the different types of cone snails include a range of different types of toxins, which may be specific for different ion channels, thus creating a venom capable of widespread nerve function interruption. One of the unique forms of these toxins is compound “X”, which is highly specific for calcium channels. Compound X is capable of long term binding to and inhibition of voltage-dependent calcium channels located in the membranes of neurons but not those of muscle cells. Identify compound X from the following:

Compound “X” is one of the most potent known neurotoxins. The toxicity of compound X was highlighted with the death of the inorganic chemist Karen Wetterhahn of Dartmouth College in 1997. After spilling no more than a few drops of compound X on her latex-glove, the barrier was immediately compromised and within seconds compound X was absorbed into the back of her hand, quickly circulating and resulting in her death ten months later. Identify compound X from the following:

Metformin, marketed under the tradename Glucophage among others, is the first-line medication for the treatment of type 2 diabetes. This is particularly true in people who are overweight. It is also used in the treatment of polycystic ovary syndrome. Metformin decreases hyperglycemia primarily by suppressing glucose production by the liver (hepatic gluconeogenesis).Chronic metformin use results in compound “X” malabsorption. Pernicious anemia is characterized by compound X deficiency caused by the absence of intrinsic factor, which complexes with compound X to facilitate absorption. In patients treated with Meformin, compound X deficiency may present without anemia and present with a peripheral neuropathy, which is often misdiagnosed as diabetic neuropathy. Identify compound X from the following:

text: The _____ and external adnexa of the eye, including the conjunctiva (the delicate membranes covering the inner surface of the eyelids and the exposed surface of the sclera) and eyelids are often exposed directly to chemicals (i.e., acids, bases, solvents), gases and particles, and drugs.

The first site of action for compounds contacting the external surface of the eye is the tear film—a three layered structure with both hydrophobic and hydrophilic properties. The outermost tear film layer is a thin (0.1 μm) hydrophobic layer that is secreted by the _____ (sebaceous) glands. This superficial lipid layer protects the underlying thicker (7 μm) aqueous layer that is produced by the _____ glands. The third layer, which has both hydrophobic and hydrophilic properties, is the very thin (0.02 to 0.05 μm) mucoid layer. It is secreted by the _____ of the conjunctiva and acts as an interface between the hydrophilic layer of the tears and the hydrophobic layer of the corneal epithelial cells. Thus, the aqueous layer is the largest portion of the tear film, and therefore water-soluble chemical compounds more readily mix with the tears and gain access to the cornea. However, a large proportion of the compounds that are splashed into the eyes is washed away by the tears and thus not absorbed.

The _____, an avascular tissue, is considered the external barrier to the internal ocular structures. It is approximately 500 μm thick, has several distinct layers, or barriers, through which a chemical must pass in order to reach the anterior chamber. The first is the epithelium. It is a stratified squamous, nonkeratinized, and multicellular hydrophobic layer. The primary barrier to chemical penetration of this avascular tissue is the set of tight junctions at the superficial layer of the epithelial cells. Thus, the permeability of this epithelium as a whole is low and only lipid soluble chemicals readily pass through this layer.

_____ separate(s) the epithelium from the stroma. The corneal stroma makes up 90% of the corneal thickness and is composed of water, collagen, and glycosaminoglycans. It contains approximately 200 lamellae, each about 1.5 to 2.0 μm thick. Due to the composition and structure of the stroma, hydrophilic chemicals easily dissolve in this thick layer, which can also act as a reservoir for these chemicals.

The inner edge of the corneal stroma is bounded by a thin, limiting basement membrane, called _____, which is secreted by the corneal endothelium. The innermost layer of the cornea, the corneal endothelium, is composed of a single layer of large diameter hexagonal cells connected by terminal bars and surrounded by lipid membranes. The endothelial cells have a relatively low ionic conductance through apical cell surface and a high-resistance paracellular pathway. Although, the permeability of the corneal endothelial cells to ionized chemicals is relatively low, it is still 100 to 200 times more permeable than the corneal epithelium. The Na+,K+-pump is located on the basolateral membrane while the energy-dependent Na+,HCO3- transporter is located on the apical membrane.

There are two separate vascular systems in the eye: (1) the _____ blood vessels, which include the vascular beds of the iris, ciliary body, and choroid, and (2) the _____ vessels. In humans, the ocular vessels are derived from the ophthalmic artery, which is a branch of the _____ artery.

The ophthalmic artery branches into (1) the central retinal artery, which enters the eye and then further branches into four major vessels serving each of the retinal quadrants; (2) two posterior ciliary arteries; and (3) several anterior arteries. In the anterior segment of the eye, there is a blood–aqueous barrier that has relatively tight junctions between the endothelial cells of the iris capillaries and nonpigmented cells of the ciliary epithelium. The major function of the ciliary epithelium is the production of _____ from the plasma filtrate present in the stroma of the ciliary processes.

text: The endothelial cells of capillaries of the retinal vessels have tight junctions similar to those that form the blood–brain barrier in the cerebral capillaries. These capillaries form the blood-retinal barrier and under normal physiologic conditions, they are largely impermeable to chemicals such as glucose and amino acids. However, at the level of the _____, the blood-retinal barrier lacks these tight-junction types of capillaries and thus hydrophilic molecules can enter the optic nerve head by diffusion from the extravascular space and cause selective damage at this site of action.

Following systemic exposure to drugs and chemicals by the oral, inhalation, dermal, or parenteral route, these compounds are distributed to all parts of the eye by the blood in the uveal blood vessels and retinal vessels. Most of these drugs and chemicals can rapidly equilibrate with the extravascular space of the choroid where they are separated from the retina and vitreous body by the retinal pigment epithelium and endothelial cells of the retinal capillaries, respectively. Hydrophilic molecules with molecular weights less than _____ Da can cross the ciliary epithelium and iris capillaries and enter the aqueous humor.

The corneal endothelium—the cells responsible for maintaining normal hydration and transparency of the corneal stroma—could be exposed to chemical compounds by the aqueous humor and limbal capillaries. Similarly, the anterior surface of the lens can also be exposed as a result of its contact with the aqueous humor. The most likely retinal target sites following systemic drug and chemical exposure appear to be the _____ and _____ in the distal retina because the endothelial cells of the choriocapillaries are permeable to proteins smaller than 50 to 70 kDa. However, the cells of the retinal pigment epithelium are joined on their basolateral surface by tight junctions—zonula occludens—that limit the passive penetration of large molecules into the neural retina.

The presence of intraocular _____ plays a special role in ocular toxicology. First, it is found in several different locations in the eye: pigmented cells of the iris, ciliary body, retinal pigment epithelium, and uveal tract. Second, it has a high binding affinity for polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, electrophiles, calcium, and toxic heavy metals such as aluminum, iron, lead, and mercury.

Although the presence of the substance from question #21 may initially play a protective role, it also results in the excessive accumulation, long-term storage, and slow release of numerous drugs and chemicals. For example, _____ binds more avidly to pigmented irides (plural form of iris) and thus its duration of action is prolonged. In addition, the accumulation of _____ in the retinal pigment epithelium produces an 80-fold higher concentration in the retina relative to liver. Similarly, _____ accumulates in the human retina such that its concentration is 5 to 750 times that in other ocular tissues.

_____ and _____—previously known as aryl hydrocarbon hydroxylase activity—are found in all bovine and mouse ocular tissues except the lens and can be induced by 3-methylcholanthrene and β-naphthoflavone.
A CYP family member—_____ in the mouse and _____ in the rabbit—is present in corneal epithelium and can be induced by phenobarbital and the peroxisome proliferator clofibrate.

The corneal epithelial CYP monooxygenase from question #24 metabolizes _____ to two of its major metabolites: 12(R)-HETE [12(R)-hydroxy-5,8,10,14-eicosatrienoic acid] and 12(R)-HETrE [12(R)-hydroxy-5,8,14-eicosatrienoic acid]. In the corneal epithelium, _____ is a potent inhibitor of Na+,K+-ATPase, whereas _____ is a potent angiogenic and chemotactic factor.

The cornea is transparent to wavelengths of light ranging between _____ nm (UV) to _____ nm (IR) in wavelengths. Exposure to UV light below this range can damage the cornea. It is most sensitive to wavelengths of approximately 270 nm. Excessive UV exposure leads to photokeratitis and corneal pathology, the classic example being welder’s arc burns.

_____ agents are able to rapidly penetrate the ocular tissues. This is particularly true for ammonia, which has been measured in the aqueous humor just seconds after application to the cornea. The toxicity of these substances is a function of their pH, being more toxic with _____ pH values. As with acid burns, the concentration of the solution and the duration of contact with the eye are important determinants of the eventual clinical outcome. Rapid and extensive irrigation after exposure and removal of particles, if present, is the immediate therapy of choice

A feature of _____ burns that differentiates them from _____ burns is that two phases of injury may be observed. There is an acute phase from exposure up to 1 week. Depending on the extent of injury, direct damage from exposure is observed in the cornea, adnexia, and possibly in the iris, ciliary body, and lens. The presence of strong _____ ions causes rapid necrosis of the corneal epithelium and, if sufficient amounts are present, penetration through and/or destruction of the successive corneal layers. Approximately, 2 to 3 weeks after, however, damaging ulceration of the corneal stroma often occurs.

Surfactants have water-soluble (hydrophilic) properties at one end of the molecule and lipophilic properties at the other end that help to dissolve fatty substances in water and also serve to reduce water surface tension. Many of these agents may be irritating or injurious to the eye. The hydrophilic portion of these compounds may be anionic, cationic, or neutral. In general, the _____ substances tend to be stronger irritants and more injurious than the other types, and _____ compounds more so than _____ ones. Because these compounds are by design soluble in both aqueous and lipid media, they readily penetrate the sandwiched aqueous and lipid barriers of the cornea. This property has implications in drug delivery; for example, low concentrations of the preservative benzalkonium chloride to ophthalmic solutions enhance ocular penetration of topically applied medications.

Accidental exposure to chemical “X” results in cortical cataracts and retinal degeneration. Chemical X itself is not cataractogenic; instead, a metabolite is the cataract-inducing agent. Aldose reductase in the rat lens is a major protein associated with chemical X dihydrodiol dehydrogenase activity and that lens aldose reductase is the enzyme responsible for the formation of the cataract-inducing agent. In addition, in vivo and in vitro studies have shown that aldose reductase inhibitors prevent chemical X-induced cataracts. Finally, there is a difference in chemical X-induced cataract formation between albino and pigmented rats, with the latter showing a faster onset and more uniform cataract. Identify chemical X from the following:

It has been known since the 1950s that schizophrenics receiving “X” drugs as anti-psychotic medication develop pigmented deposits in their eyes and skin. The pigmentation begins as tiny deposits on the anterior surface of the lens and progresses, with increasing dose, to involve the cornea as well. The “X” drugs combine with melanin to form a photosensitive product that reacts with sunlight, causing formation of the deposits. The amount of pigmentation is related to the dose of the drug, with the annual yearly dose being the most predictive dose metric. More recent epidemiologic evidence demonstrates a dose-related increase in the risk of cataracts from use of “X”-like drugs, including both antipsychotic drugs such as chlorpromazine and nonantipsychotic “X” drugs. Identify the class of drugs represented by “X” from the following:

The cardiac glycosides digoxin (Lanoxin) and digitoxin (Crystodigin) are digitalis derivatives used in the treatment of congestive heart disease and in certain cardiac arrhythmias. As part of the extract of the plant foxglove, digitalis was recommended for heart failure (dropsy) over 200 years ago. Digitalis induced visual system abnormalities such as decreased vision, flickering scotomas, and altered color vision were documented during that time (i.e., 1785). Approximately, 20–60% of patients with cardiac glycoside serum levels in the therapeutic range and 50–80% of the patients with cardiac glycoside serum levels in the toxic range complain of visual system disturbances within 2 weeks after the onset of therapy. The most frequent visual complaints are color vision impairments and hazy or snowy vision, although complaints of flickering light, colored spots surrounded by bright halos, blurred vision, and glare sensitivity also are reported. Digoxin produces more toxicity than digitoxin due to its:

options.text: greater Vd and plasma protein binding
lower Vd and plasma protein binding

text: Drug “X” is a cGMP-specific phosphodiesterase (PDE) type 5 inhibitor that is utilized in the treatment of erectile dysfunction. This drug is also a weak cGMP PDE type 6 inhibitor, which is present in rod and cone photoreceptors. Transient visual symptoms such as a blue tinge to vision, increased brightness of lights and blurry vision as well as alterations in scotopic and photopic ERGs have been reported following drug “X” usage. Identify drug X from the following:

Drug “X”, a triphenylethylene derivative, is a nonsteroidal anti-estrogenic drug that competes with estrogen for its receptor sites. It is a highly effective antitumor agent used for the treatment of metastatic breast carcinoma in postmenopausal women. Drug “X”-induced retinopathy following chronic high-dose therapy (180 to 240 mg per day for approximately 2 years) was first reported 20 years ago. At this dose, there is widespread axonal degeneration in the macular and perimacular area, as evidenced by the presence of different sized yellow–white refractile opacities in the inner plexiform layer and nerve fiber layer observed during fundus examination. Macular edema may or may not be present. Clinical symptoms include a permanent decrease in visual acuity and abnormal visual fields, as the axonal degeneration is irreversible. Identify drug “X” from the following:

Clinical manifestations from exposure to chemical “X” include amblyopia, blindness, optic neuritis or atrophy, peripheral and central scotomas, paralysis of eye muscles, and decreased visual function. Moderate to high level chemical “X” exposures produce scotopic and temporal visual system deficits in occupationally exposed factory workers and developmentally exposed monkeys and rats. Identify chemical “X” from the following:
Which of the following is TRUE of ocular effects of drug abuse?

options.text: Corneal defects have resulted from smoking "crack" cocaine
Talc retinopathy can occur after a single use of an intravenous drug
Miosis from opioids results from inhibition of papillary dilation

text: Human and nonhuman primates are highly sensitive to methanol-induced neurotoxicity due to their limited capacity to oxidize formic acid. The toxicity occurs in several stages. It first occurs as a mild CNS depression, followed by an asymptomatic 12- to 24-hour latent period, then by a syndrome consisting of formic acidemia, uncompensated metabolic acidosis, ocular and visual toxicity, coma, and possibly death. The treatment of methanol poisoning involves both combating acidosis and preventing methanol oxidation. Formic acid is the toxic metabolite that mediates the metabolic acidosis as well as the retinal and optic nerve toxicity observed in humans, monkeys, and rats with a decreased capacity for folate metabolism. Which enzyme is responsible for difference susceptibility of humans and rats for methanol toxicity?

text: The primary contractile unit within the heart is the cardiac _____. These cells are composed of several major structural features and organelles. A primary component is the contractile elements known as the _____. Each of these components consists of a number of smaller filaments (the thick and thin myofilaments). The thick filaments are special assemblies of the protein _____, whereas the thin filaments are made up primarily of the protein _____.

Under electron microscopy, the structural components of myocardial contractile proteins display alternating dark bands (A bands, predominantly composed of _____) and light bands (I bands, predominantly composed of _____). Visible in the middle of the I band is a dense vertical Z line. The area between two Z lines is called a sarcomere, the fundamental unit of muscle contraction. Although cardiac and skeletal muscle share many similarities, a major difference lies in the organization of cardiac muscle cells into a functional syncytium where cardiac muscle cells are joined end-to-end by dense structures known as _____. Within these, there are tight gap junctions that facilitate action potential propagation and intercellular communication. About 50% of each cardiac muscle cell is composed of contractile elements. The rest of the intracellular space contains the remaining major components of the cell: mitochondria (33%), one or more nuclei (5%), the sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) (2%), lysosomes (very low), glycogen granules, a Golgi network, and cytosol (12%).

Cardiac _____ is the largest cell in the heart and contributes to the majority of cardiac mass. However, these cells make up only about one-quarter of all the cells in the heart. Cardiac fibroblasts, vascular cells, Purkinje cells, and other connective tissue cells make up the majority of cell number in the heart. Cardiac _____ make up approximately 90% of these “nonmuscle” cells. Cardiac muscle cells are generally considered to be terminally differentiated. These cells may be multinucleated, but they may not divide after birth unless under certain circumstances in some species such as mice.

Cardiac muscle cells produce an action potential when activated. In the resting state, the resting potential of a myocyte is about −60 to −90 mV relative to the extracellular fluid potential. A sudden depolarization changes the membrane potential from _____ inside to _____ inside, followed by a repolarization to reset the resting potential.

The process of an action potential from depolarization to the completion of repolarization is divided into five phases in cardiac Purkinje fibers. Phase 0 represents a rapid depolarization due to the inward current of _____. Phase 1 is associated with an immediate rapid repolarization, during which the inward current of this ion is inactivated and a transient _____ outward current is activated, followed by an action potential plateau or phase 2, which is dominated by slowly decreasing inward _____ current and a slow activation of an outward _____ current. Phase 3 reflects a fast _____ outward current and inactivation of the plateau _____ inward current, and phase 4 is the diastolic interval for the resetting of resting potential.
